Long ago, before the mountains had names, the sky was a heavy blanket of grey slate that rested directly upon the earth. Humans walked hunched over, crawling on their bellies like lizards, unable to stand tall. The birds had no room to fly, so they hopped angrily in the dust.

A young girl named Elara, tired of scraping her knees against the rocks, found a long, sturdy pole of ironwood. She wedged it between the ground and the slate sky. With a grunt that shook the roots of the trees, she pushed. The sky groaned, heavy and stubborn, but it rose an inch. Encouraged, the village joined her. Strong men, wise elders, and laughing children all pushed against the pole.

Day by day, they heaved. As the sky lifted, the first sunbeam slipped through, painting the world in gold. The birds, finally free, soared upward, their wings beating against the grey until it shattered into blue fragments.

When the sky was finally high enough, Elara planted the ironwood pole deep into the earth to hold it there forever. Over centuries, the wood turned to stone and grew vast forests upon its slopes. Today, we call that pole "Mount Everest," the pillar that holds up the heavens so we might walk with our heads held high.
